page: Amazon Web Services r8idb.16xlarge is a memory-optimized eighth-generation instance powered by the Nitro hypervisor. It features 64 dedicated vCPUs built on the x86_64 architecture using Intel processors, with 32 physical cores running at a clock speed of 3.9 GHz and supporting 2.0 threads per core. The instance is equipped with 512.0 GB of system memory, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core to support memory-intensive workloads.

For local storage, the r8idb.16xlarge includes 3800 GB of high-speed NVMe SSD instance store volumes. This local storage is bundled directly with the instance, offering low-latency data access. Network performance is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 67 Gbps, facilitating rapid data transfers across distributed environments. No GPUs are included in this configuration.

From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the r8idb.16xlarge balances high-density memory allocation with dedicated compute resources and fast local NVMe storage, reducing the need for external network-attached storage for temporary data. This hardware profile is optimized for workloads such as large-scale databases, in-memory caching, real-time analytics, and enterprise applications requiring high memory capacity and fast local storage.
description: Amazon Web Services r8idb.16xlarge is a memory-optimized, eighth-generation instance designed for resource-intensive enterprise workloads. Powered by the Nitro hypervisor, it features 64 dedicated vCPUs on an x86_64 Intel architecture with 32 physical cores operating at 3.9 GHz. The system is equipped with 512.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per core. Storage is addressed via 3800 GB of bundled local NVMe SSD instance store volumes, providing high-speed, low-latency data access. Network connectivity is supported by a baseline bandwidth of 67 Gbps. Lacking GPU acceleration, this instance is engineered for high-density memory and storage performance. It is highly suited for large-scale databases, in-memory data grids, real-time data analytics, and memory-intensive enterprise applications.
